Row1.Rationale: PD‑1/PD‑L1 blockade restores IFN‑γ–driven JAK‑STAT signaling in T cells, enhancing cytotoxic activity and promoting durable responses to PD‑1 inhibitors such as pembrolizumab in PD‑L1–positive NSCLC.[3,6,8]

Row2.Rationale: High immune‑active signatures (e.g., T‑cell–inflamed/TNF‑related gene sets) and inflamed microenvironments correlate with better outcomes to PD‑1/PD‑L1 inhibitors in NSCLC, consistent with enhanced TNF‑mediated effector function after PD‑1 blockade.[2,3,6]

Row3.Pathway ID/Name: hsa04650: Natural killer cell mediated cytotoxicity (KEGG)
Row3.Rationale: PD‑1 inhibitors augment NK and CD8⁺ T‑cell cytotoxicity; NSCLC tumors with higher baseline cytotoxic/immune infiltration signatures derive greater benefit from PD‑1–directed immunotherapy.[2,3,6,8]

Row4.Rationale: EGFR‑TKIs can increase MHC I/II expression and antigen presentation; a more antigen‑visible tumor microenvironment supports improved recognition and killing once PD‑1 is blocked by pembrolizumab.[8]

Row5.Rationale: dMMR/MSI‑H NSCLC exhibits high proliferation and mutation burden, increasing neoantigen load; such tumors show enriched anti‑tumor immunity and higher responsiveness to PD‑1/PD‑L1 inhibitors.[6]

Row6.Rationale: Loss of MMR function (dMMR/MSI‑H) increases tumor mutational burden and neoantigenicity, associated with higher CD4⁺ memory and CD8⁺ T‑cell infiltration and improved responses to ICIs in lung cancer.[6]

Row7.Pathway ID/Name: hsa05235: PD‑L1 expression and PD‑1 checkpoint pathway in cancer (KEGG)
Row7.Rationale: Very high tumor PD‑L1 expression (≥90%) in advanced NSCLC is associated with significantly longer overall survival on first‑line pembrolizumab versus PD‑L1 50–89%, reflecting stronger dependence on PD‑1/PD‑L1 signaling and greater benefit from its blockade.[4,6,8]

Row8.Pathway ID/Name: hsa05202: Transcriptional misregulation in cancer (KEGG; EGFR/NOX4–IL‑8–PD‑L1 axis)
Row8.Rationale: In EGFR‑mutant NSCLC, NOX4/YY1‑driven IL‑8/PD‑L1 upregulation and other TKI‑resistance mechanisms create an immunosuppressive microenvironment with high PD‑L1 yet poor ICI efficacy, contributing to relative resistance to PD‑1 inhibitors.[8]

Row9.Pathway ID/Name: hsa05200: Pathways in cancer (KEGG; EGFR signaling)
Row9.Rationale: EGFR‑driven NSCLC activates PD‑1/PD‑L1 signaling and multiple immunosuppressive mechanisms, leading to low response rates to ICIs despite PD‑L1 expression; EGFR‑mutant subtypes show limited benefit from PD‑1 blockade compared with oncogene‑negative NSCLC.[5,8]

Row10.Rationale: Oncogenic signaling (e.g., via EGFR and downstream PI3K‑Akt/mTOR) is linked to immune evasion in NSCLC and contributes to primary resistance to both TKIs and ICIs, supporting reduced sensitivity to pembrolizumab in these molecular contexts.[3,8]

Row11.Pathway ID/Name: hsa05206: MicroRNAs in cancer (KEGG; stemness/CSC programs)
Row11.Rationale: NSCLC cancer‑stem–cell–associated pathways (e.g., Wnt/β‑catenin, STAT3, mTOR) maintain immune‑evasive, chemoresistant phenotypes; such stemness programs are associated with poor responses to systemic therapy, including immunotherapy.[3]

Row12.Pathway ID/Name: hsa04611: Platelet activation / inflammatory response (KEGG surrogate for immune‑active TME)
Row12.Rationale: A 9‑gene immune‑chemotherapy prediction score derived from NSCLC trials is enriched for immune‑active/myeloid‑activation pathways; high‑score tumors show inflamed microenvironments and superior benefit from PD‑1/PD‑L1–based regimens relative to low‑score tumors.[2]
